coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots with high brightness and stability towards efficient Cr(VI) and l-arginine sensors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xuanqi
Zhang, Xiao
Wang, Jianrong
Liu, Chenchen
Li, Chuanhao
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: To create fluorescent sensors for Cr (VI) and l-arginine detection, blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs, named as M-CDs) were prepared via a solvothermal synthesis by using m-phenylenediamine. The introduction of sulfuric acid changed the blue-emitting of CDs into green-emitting (named as S-CDs), in which the photoluminescence (PL) peak wavelengths of samples M-CDs and S-CDs were 450 and 540 nm, while their PL efficiencies were 21.7 and 84.2 % respectively. Sulfuric acid resulted in the average fluorescent lifetime of the CDs was prolonged into 8.19 from 4.65 ns. The photo and thermal stable tests demonstrated that theses CDs revealed excellent stability. Meanwhile, CDs flexible films with bright blue- and green-emitting were prepared utilizing the favorable solubility of CDs in polar solvents. Mono-dispersed CDs as luminescent materials demonstrated it promising prospects in luminescent devices field. Furthermore, M-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity towards Cr(VI) and l-arginine. The detected limit (0.86 μM for Cr(VI) and 0.43 μM for l-arginine) was determined by assessing the response of M-CDs to various concentrations of Cr(VI) ion and l-arginine solutions. These bright CDs revealed great potential for accurate detection of Cr(VI) and l-arginine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Sensor
Cr (Ⅵ)
L-arginine
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) and l-arginine in lake water
Fabrication of CDs emitting conversion layer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Function of China, PR China, (grant no. 51972145), the Independent Innovation Team from Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, Jinan, China (grant no, 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of M-CDs and S-CDs samples.
(a-h) TEM and HRTEM images of sample S-CDs. (i) Size distribution of sample S-CDs. The particle size distribution was assessed by analyzing 100 particles.
(a) XPS full spectra, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s and (e) S 2p spectra of S-CDs sample. (f) FTIR spectra of samples M-CDs and S-CDs.

(a, b) UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of M-CDs and S-CDs samples. (c) Fluorescent decay plots of M-CDs and S-CDs samples. (d, e) PL evolution of samples M-CDs and S-CDs with time under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. (g, h) PL evolution of samples M-CDs and S-CDs with temperature. (f, i) PL intensity evolution of samples M-CDs and S-CDs before and after UV light irradiation and heat treatment. Insets in (a, b) show the pictures of samples M-CDs and S-CDs under room light and UV light irradiation.
(a, c) PL spectra of M-CDs samples in Cr (VI) and l-arginine at different concentrations. (b, d) Linear relationship of F/F0 with Cr (VI) and l-arginine concentrations.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots with high brightness and stability towards efficient Cr(VI) and l-arginine sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Facult of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St., 31-155 Krakow, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Facult of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Facult of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Cracow University of Technology, Warszawska 24 St., 31-155 Krakow, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To create fluorescent sensors for Cr (VI) and l-arginine detection, blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs, named as M-CDs) were prepared via a solvothermal synthesis by using m-phenylenediamine. The introduction of sulfuric acid changed the blue-emitting of CDs into green-emitting (named as S-CDs), in which the photoluminescence (PL) peak wavelengths of samples M-CDs and S-CDs were 450 and 540 nm, while their PL efficiencies were 21.7 and 84.2 % respectively. Sulfuric acid resulted in the average fluorescent lifetime of the CDs was prolonged into 8.19 from 4.65 ns. The photo and thermal stable tests demonstrated that theses CDs revealed excellent stability. Meanwhile, CDs flexible films with bright blue- and green-emitting were prepared utilizing the favorable solubility of CDs in polar solvents. Mono-dispersed CDs as luminescent materials demonstrated it promising prospects in luminescent devices field. Furthermore, M-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity towards Cr(VI) and l-arginine. The detected limit (0.86 μM for Cr(VI) and 0.43 μM for l-arginine) was determined by assessing the response of M-CDs to various concentrations of Cr(VI) ion and l-arginine solutions. These bright CDs revealed great potential for accurate detection of Cr(VI) and l-arginine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) as zero-dimensional luminescent nanomaterials follows that of semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, with it dimensions measuring less than 10 nm [1]. The easy availability of starting materials, simple synthesis method, high photoluminescence (PL) efficiency, wide tunable spectrum, and biocompatibility have rendered CDs highly attractive. Consequently, the CDs is widely used in optoelectronic applications [2], biological imaging [3,4], drug delivery [5], sensing [6,7] and other fields. The CDs can serve as fluorescent probes in the field of detection. In fact, CDs fluorescence probes could be used as pH, molecule, ion, and contaminant sensors [8–10]. The detected analyte can serve as a fluorescence quenching agent, inducing fluorescence quenching of CDs. The fluorescence quenching may occur when a quencher reacts with the surface functional groups on CDs. The quenching mechanism of CDs can be attributed to the static quenching effect (SQE) and inner filter effect (IFE).

With industrial development, the issue of heavy metal ion pollution had emerged as a significant societal concern. The high toxicity of Cr(Ⅵ), Pb2+ and other heavy metal ions poses a significant environmental threat. The pollution of groundwater by heavy metal ions not only harms the soil but also threatens the health of organisms. Excessive intake of Cr(Ⅵ) ion can result in the development of lung cancer, nasopharyngeal cancer, tuberculosis, and potentially lead to organ failure. In 2017, the World Health Organization (WHO) classified chromium metal as a class III carcinogen. WHO limited the threshold limit of Cr ions in drinking water at 0.05 mg/L [11]. At present, atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry and other methods were extensively employed for heavy metals detection [12]. However, these approaches require sophisticated analytical instruments and complex operating procedures. Therefore, Cr(VI) could be quickly and accurately determined by using the unique luminescence characteristics of CDs as a fluorescence probe. The detected limit was relatively low. Currently, many literatures extensively reported the utilization of CDs for Cr(Ⅵ) detection. Hahn et al. successfully employed blue-emitting CDs to detect Cr(VI), achieving a remarkable detected limit was 0.6 μM [13]. Wang et al. synthesized B and N co-doped blue emission CDs with PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of 59.01 % [14]. The fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by Cr(VI) could be ascribed to the synergistic effects of SQE and IFE. The detected limit was 0.24 μM. CDs had promising application prospects in Cr (VI) sensitive detection.

L-arginine, as a basic amino acid compound, was a necessary substance in the maintenance of normal physiological processes. L-arginine promotes ammonia conversion, a toxic substance produced by the body, into non-toxic urea. Furthermore, l-arginine affects various physiological processes such as cellular proliferation, wound repair, immune response regulation, protein biosynthesis and hormone secretion [15,16]. The excess or lack of l-arginine could affect the health of an organism. Therefore, the detection of arginine is necessary. The detection of l-arginine has been extensively reported, and the techniques employed were becoming increasingly mature. Currently, high performance liquid chromatography and gas chromatography are the predominant methods utilized for detection [17,18]. However, these detection methods were costly and complex to operate, requiring professional instruments and equipment. The development of a highly selective approach for l-arginine detection is therefore imperative. Papers have reported on CDs detection of l-arginine. For example, Lu et al. prepared blue-emitting CDs using microwave method to detect l-arginine, the detected limit was 0.26 μM [19]. Wang et al. prepared CDs with dual-emission fluorescence via the hydrothermal method [20]. l-arginine could be detected in an aqueous solution through a proportional fluorescence method, with a detected limit of 9.16 μM. Mi et al. designed a highly selective fluorescence sensor for highly selective of dopamine, lysine, and l-arginine detection [21]. The detected limit of l-arginine was 26 μM.

In this study, the blue-emitting CDs (named as M-CDs) was successfully synthesized using m-phenylenediamine as the precursor material. The green-emitting CDs (named as S-CDs) was synthesized using sulfuric acid. The PL peaks were 450 and 500 nm, respectively. The PL intensity and PLQYs of acid-assisted S-CDs were significantly enhanced, resulting in an increase in the PLQYs from 21.7 to 84.2 %. M-CDs and S-CDs samples exhibited narrower full width at half maximum (FWHM) of PL spectra of 54.40 and 54.32 nm. The fluorescence lifetimes of M-CDs and S-CDs were 4.65 and 8.19 ns. The introduction of S improved the PL properties of CDs and resulted in narrow PL spectra and high PLQYs. The change of fluorescent lifetime suggested the adjustment of surface situation of the CDs because of S components. The experimental data further substantiated improved PL properties and stability of CDs through doping with S element. The CDs were used to create flexible films because of their bright luminescence and excellent stability. It is found the CDs are crucial for further application in lighting field. In Addition, through the detection of various cations, anions, and amino acid molecules, it was determined that M-CDs possess the capability to effectively detect Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine. The detected limit was 0.86 and 0.43 μM, respectively. The luminescence quenching of M-CDs was attributed to SQE and IFE.

M-phenylenediamine (m-PD, 99.5 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), and L-serine (99 %) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethanol (EtOH, 99.7 %) was supplied by Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, MW = 44.05) and glutathione (reduced, 98 %) were purchased from Aladdin. L-arginine (98 %), l-proline (99 %), and l-tryptophan (99 %) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Glycine (99 %) was taken from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. L-phenylalanine (98.5 %) was obtained by Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. The deionized water (DI water) was got from a Millipore system (SYNSV0000, France).

Typically, 10 mg of m-PD was dissolved in 20 mL EtOH, and the solute was uniformly dispersed in the solvent by ultrasound for 10 min. Then, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The solution cooled to room temperature was passed through a filter of 0.22 μm to obtain a blue-emitting CDs solution. The sample was named M-CDs.

The synthesis of sample S-CDs was like to that of M-CDs, except for different starting materials. 10 mg of m-PD and 3 mL (0.5 mol/L) of H2SO4 were dissolved in 20 mL of EtOH, and subsequent processing steps were the same as M-CDs. The CDs solution with green-emitting was named as S-CDs.

The detection for Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine using M-CDs were as follows. 1 mL of Cr(Ⅵ) ion or l-arginine solutions were mixed with 10 μL of M-CDs and stored at room temperature for 10 min. The concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) or l-arginine solution was 0 to 300 μM. The PL spectra of samples were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 395 nm.

The impact of M-CDs on metal ions (Mg2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CH3COO−), and amino acid molecules including l-phenylalanine, glycine, glutathione, l-arginine, l-proline, l-tryptophan, and L-serine was investigated. The solution concentration was 1 mM. The follow-up procedure remains unchanged from the aforementioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) and l-arginine in lake water
ce:section.ce:para: Lake water was used to verify the detection effect of M-CDs in real sample. The lake water utilized in the experiment was sourced from University of Jinan, with impurities effectively eliminated through a filter of 0.11 μm then used directly. Then, the concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine in real sample were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs emitting conversion layer
ce:section.ce:para: 1.5 g of PVA was dispersed in 20 mL DI water, heated and stirred to prepare PVA solution. M-CDs and S-CDs solutions were thoroughly mixed with the PVA solution through stirring. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was applied onto a clean glass plate and air-dried at room temperature for 5 h. Finally, the luminous transition layer of blue- and green-emitting were obtained.

The morphology of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Talos F200S, America). The elemental composition of CDs was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+, America). The functional group analysis of CDs was conducted using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 380, America). The absorption spectra of samples were measured by Hitachi U-4100 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Japan). The PL spectra of CDs samples were determined by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Japan). The fluorescence decay plots of samples were analyzed using a Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Japan). The CDs samples were irradiated with Shanghai Chitang Industry WFH-204B UV analyzer (China).

Fig. 1 shows the preparation procedure of CDs. Bright blue-emitting CDs (M-CDs) were prepared by a solvothermal method with m-PD as precursor at 180 °C for 12 h. The synthesis of green-emitting CDs (S-CDs) was conducted by incorporating sulfuric acid as a dopant, while maintaining the unchanged synthesis conditions. The incorporation of S element the luminescent color of CDs and enhanced PL intensity. Fig. 2 a-2h shows the TEM and HRTEM images of sample S-CDs with different magnification. Sample S-CDs exhibited excellent dispersity in EtOH. S-CDs nanoparticles possessed a spherical morphology and displayed uniform size distribution. The results shown that S-CDs doped with sulfuric acid conformed to the morphology characteristics of CDs nanoparticles. Fig. 2f-2h shows the HRTEM images of nanoparticles with different S-CDs. S-CDs nanoparticles have different crystalline morphology. The HRTEM images in Fig. 2f to 2h show clearly the lattice fringes of S-CDs. The lattice fringe spacing was measured at 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane spacing [22]. S-CDs exhibited fine crystallinity. The average diameter of S-CDs was estimated to be 4.25 ± 2.5 nm (in Fig. 2i).

The element and microstructure of CDs were analyzed through XPS and FTIR. Firstly, the S-CDs composition was evaluated via XPS. Fig. 3 a shows that S-CDs primarily consisted of elements C, N, O, and S. The characteristic peaks corresponding to the elements C, N, O, and S were observed at 168, 284, 399, and 531 eV respectively, and element molar ratios were 71.91, 10.13, 14.57, and 3.39 %, respectively. Then, the spectral patterns of different elements were fitted by peaks. Fig. 3b shows the spectrum of C 1s, with peaks at 284.8, 285.5, and 288.6 eV corresponding to the C-C/CC bond, C-S/C-N/C-O bond, and CN/CO bond, respectively. The observed peaks of the N 1s electron binding energy in Fig. 3c at energies of 399.8, 401.8 and 403.9 eV could be attributed to the presence of Pyridine N, Pyrrole N, and Graphite N bonds. Fig. 3d shows the spectrum of O 1s, with C=O and C-O peaks observed at 531.3 and 532.5 eV. Fig. 3e displays the S 2p spectrum, indicating S-CDs has −C-SO2, −C-SO3, and −C-SO4 bonding, with characteristic peaks at 166.2, 168.3 and 169.6 eV, respectively [23–25].

The FTIR spectra of CDs samples revealed the surface functional groups composition (Fig. 3f). The vibration band observed at 3300–3550 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H bonds, indicating the abundant presence of amino and carboxyl groups in M-CDs and S-CDs samples. This provided evidence for the excellent solubility of CDs in polar solvents. The stretching vibrations of the C-H bond and the CO bond correspond to the vibrational bands at 2800–3000 cm-1 and 1600–1750 cm-1, respectively. Compared with M-CDs sample, the FTIR spectra of S-CDs sample exhibited a new stretching vibration band. The observed vibration bands observed at 1230 and 1070 cm-1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of the C-S and −SO3H bonds [26]. The experimental results demonstrated the successful doping of S element in S-CDs. The S element was successfully doped into the network of CDs by the green-emitting S-CDs assisted by sulfuric acid. The PLQYs of S-CDs sample exhibited a significant enhancement by adding S element. The experimental results demonstrated the introduction of non-metallic dopant S could effectively increase PL intensity and PLQYs of CDs.

The absorption spectra of M-CDs shows that the absorption peaks at 285 and 355 nm were attributed to the π−π* transitions of the CC bond and n-π* transitions, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The PL peak of M-CDs was 450 nm, with the FWHM of PL spectrum of 54.40 nm. S-CDs sample exhibited a high absorption peak at 270, 420, and 470 nm (Fig. 4b). The absorption peak at 270 nm was due to the π-π* transition of the CC bond. Meanwhile, the peaks observed at 420 and 470 nm were attributed to n-π* transitions in the chemical bonds on S-CDs surface. Additionally, the fluorescence peak of S-CDs was located at 500 nm with a corresponding the FWHM of PL spectrum of 54.32 nm. Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information) shows PL spectra excited at different wavelengths for CDs samples. Notably, the PL peaks of M-CDs and S-CDs remained consistent at 450 and 500 nm. The excitation wavelength of M-CDs in H2O was 395 nm (Fig. S1c). The PLQYs of M-CDs and S-CDs samples were 21.7 % and 84.2 %. The results demonstrated the PLQYs of CDs was enhanced through the addition of sulfuric acid to the solvent (Table S1, see Supporting Information). Fig. 4a and 4b show the CDs samples in room light and ultraviolet light. CDs prepared by solvothermal method were clear and transparent solutions under sunlight and emit bright blue- and green-emitting under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence decay plots of M-CDs and S-CDs samples. The fluorescence lifetimes of M-CDs and S-CDs were fitted with a double exponential formula, which were 4.65 and 8.19 ns respectively (Table S2, see Supporting Information).

CDs samples were subsequently subjected to stability tests in order to comprehensively characterize the CDs. The light and thermal stability of a luminous material used in the field of lighting is of very importance. CDs samples were continuously exposed to UV lamp for 180 min, subsequently tested at intervals of 30 min (Fig. 4d and 4e). The PL intensity of M-CDs and S-CDs remained at a higher level after UV light irradiation, which were 1.03 and 1.02 times of the initial level respectively (Fig. 4f). Then, the thermal stability of CDs was tested. Since the solvent for CDs was EtOH, the heating temperature was up to 78 °C. As shown in Fig. 4g and 4 h, CDs samples were subjected to a temperature increase from 15 to 78 °C, and the PL spectra of CDs was recorded at intervals of every 10 °C rise in temperature. Obviously, PL intensity of S-CDs exhibited minimal variation following the thermal stability test, with a sustained intensity level of 99.5 % compared to initial value (Fig. 4i). The PL intensity of M-CDs exhibited a slight decrease, reaching 77.9 % of the initial level, while still maintaining a bright blue-emitting. In general, CDs samples exhibited excellent photostability and thermal stability. It provides powerful experimental data support for the application of M-CDs and S-CDs.

The chemical properties of CDs were further investigated, the impact of salt tolerance and different pH solutions on PL intensity of CDs was examined (Fig. S2 and S3, see Supporting Information). CDs samples were individually dissolved in NaCl solutions of varying concentrations, as shown in Fig. S2a. The experimental data indicated that PL intensity of M-CDs and S-CDs remained unaffected by a high concentration of NaCl solution. Then the effects of different pH solutions (pH = 2–13) on CDs samples were tested (Fig. S2b). Sample M-CDs remained high PL intensity in acidic solutions, in contrast a significant decline observed in the case of pH more than 7. M-CDs was more sensitive to alkaline solution, and it will be quenched. Compared to M-CDs, the PL effect of various pH solutions on S-CDs was negligible, while S-CDs still exhibited vibrant green-emitting in highly acidic and alkaline solutions. To understand the response of CDs samples to ions, CDs was dissolved in solutions of different anions, cations, and amino acids. M-CDs was sensitive to cation Cr(Ⅵ) and amino acid l-arginine (Fig. S3c). M-CDs could cause fluorescence quenching of the solution. S-CDs sample exhibited sensitivity towards Cr(Ⅵ) and Fe (III) (Fig. S3d). However, the selectivity of S-CDs for Cr(Ⅵ) and Fe (III) was found to be unsatisfactory. M-CDs was an ideal fluorescent probe material that could be utilized in the detection field to achieve visual detection of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine.

As is widely recognized, excessive Cr(Ⅵ) in the environment poses significant threats to both ecosystems and living organisms. Therefore, the detection of Cr(Ⅵ) ion was imperative. M-CDs solution was added to Cr(Ⅵ) solution with different concentration. Fig. 5 a records PL spectra of solutions with different Cr(Ⅵ) concentrations. The luminous color of M-CDs remained unchanged upon the addition of Cr(Ⅵ). However, the PL intensity of samples gradually decreased, indicating that Cr(Ⅵ) effectively quenched the fluorescence of M-CDs. Fig. 5b shows the recorded PL intensity. The concentration and PL intensity shown a good linear relationship ranging, with a corresponding correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9926 (y = -0.0035x + 0.9827). The detected limit for Cr(Ⅵ) using M-CDs can be calculated by 3δ/k, where δ represents the deviation of the response value and k represents the slope of the standard curve [27,28]. Table S3 illustrates that M-CDs exhibit a low detected limit for Cr(Ⅵ). The results demonstrated the significant potential of M-CDs in Cr(Ⅵ) ion detection [29–32].

M-CDs can also be used for the detection of l-arginine. M-CDs was added to l-arginine solution of different concentration, and recorded PL spectra of the samples. Fig. 5c shows the PL spectra of the concentration of l-arginine solution from 0 to 300 μM, and the PL intensity gradually decreases. M-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity towards l-arginine. Then, the detected limit of M-CDs for l-arginine was tested, and the correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9905 (y = -0.0070x + 1.0062). The detected limit was 0.43 μM (Fig. 5d). Table S4 illustrates a comparison for l-arginine detection with the reports in literature. Compared with other materials, the synthesis method of CDs was simple and less toxic, so it was more suitable for fluorescence probe. Therefore, M-CDs revealed high sensitivity and low detection limit for l-arginine.

The diverse interaction between CDs and the quencher corresponded to distinct fluorescence quenching mechanisms. Quenching mechanisms can be categorized as follows: SQE, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer, surface energy transfer, dexter energy transfer and IFE [33]. The PL and fluorescence lifetime of M-CDs were analyzed to investigate the quenching mechanism of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine on M-CDs. Fig. S4a shows the overlap between the absorption spectra of Cr(Ⅵ) and the PL excitation and emission spectra of M-CDs, indicating PL of M-CDs was decreased by Cr(Ⅵ), which ultimately leads to fluorescence quenching of M-CDs. The IFE effect led to the quenching of fluorescence of M-CDs [34]. Fig. S4b shows the fluorescence decay plots of M-CDs with and without Cr(Ⅵ). The fluorescence lifetime were 8.51 and 8.66 ns, respectively (Table S5, see Supporting Information). The introduction of Cr(Ⅵ) did not alter the excited state lifetime of M-CDs [35]. The quenching mechanism of Cr(Ⅵ) on M-CDs could be attributed to SQE and IFE.

Fig. S4c shows the absorption spectra of M-CDs in l-arginine detection system, and no l-arginine absorption peak is observed. The absorption peak at 390 nm exhibited a gradual decrease with the increase in l-arginine concentration, while the absorption peak at 240 nm shown a corresponding gradual increase. The introduction of l-arginine modifies the M-CDs surface functional groups, thereby influencing the absorption peak of M-CDs. When l-arginine interacts with M-CDs, the guanidine group in l-arginine formed a strong affinity with the amino or hydroxyl group of M-CDs through hydrogen bonding, thus influencing the surface state of M-CDs [36]. l-arginine disrupted the charge transfer process of M-CDs and enhanced non-radiative transition, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching of M-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of M-CDs was 8.46 ns (Fig. S4d and Table S5, see Supporting Information). Due to a small difference in fluorescence lifetime, the fluorescence of M-CDs was quenched by l-arginine through SQE.

The detection effect of M-CDs in practical samples was further examined. The applicability of M-CDs for the detection of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine in lake water was experimentally validated. PL response of M-CDs to lake water sample was detected with the spiked of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine at concentrations of 5, 10, 30, and 50 μM, respectively. As shown in Table S6, the recovery of Cr(Ⅵ) was 91.63–96.60 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.68–4.84 %. The recovery of l-arginine was 91.00–98.20 %, and the RSD was 3.25–6.81 %. The results shown that M-CDs was feasible for the detection of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine in real sample.

CDs samples prepared in this work exhibited bright blue- and green-emitting, as well as excellent photostability and thermal stability. The light-emitting flexible films exhibiting blue- and green-emission were successfully fabricated utilizing CDs. The PL emission of M- CDs was influenced by aqueous solutions. Thus, PL peak of M-CDs exhibited a redshift from 448 to 470 nm when M-CDs was dissolved in PVA solution (Fig. S5a). The S-CDs exhibited no discernible changed in response to the aqueous solution, and the PL peak of the green-emitting film remained relatively stable, with a wavelength of 500 nm (Fig. S5d). The insets in Fig. S5c and S5f shows CDs films exhibit bright luminescence and excellent flexibility. Then the stability of CDs films samples was tested. CDs films were continuously exposed to a UV lamp for 180 min, and PL spectra were recorded every 30 min (Fig. S5b and S5e). The luminescence of films remained unaffected by irradiation from an UV lamp, thus demonstrating the excellent photostability of the fluorescent films prepared in this study. The prepared flexible films were stored at room temperature and in air for 60 days, during which the PL intensity remained consistently high (Fig. S5c and S5f). The PL intensity was maintained at 97.7 and 93.8 % of the initial level, demonstrating excellent storage stability of M-CDs and S-CDs films. The experimental data provided support for the further application of light-emitting flexible films. The CIE coordinates corresponding to M-CDs and S-CDs films were (0.16, 0.26) and (0.15, 0.69) (Fig. S6a and S6b). Therefore, CDs light-emitting flexible films possess significant potential for application in the lighting industry.

Blue-emitting M-CDs were synthesized using m-PD as precursors. Green-emitting S-CDs were synthesized by introducing S element. The PL peak wavelengths of M-CDs and S-CDs were 450 and 500 nm. The incorporation of S element greatly augmented the PLQYs of CDs. The PLQYs of M-CDs and S-CDs were 21.7 % and 84.2 %, respectively. CDs samples exhibited excellent stability. Moreover, Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine were detected by M-CDs fluorescent probe. The detected limit of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine by M-CDs were significantly lower at 0.86 and 0.43 μM, respectively. The significance of M-CDs in the field of detection has been substantiated. This method was characterized by its simplicity and suitability for visual detection of Cr(Ⅵ) and l-arginine. In addition, the flexible films prepared by M-CDs and S-CDs also exhibit excellent stability. In conclusion, CDs nanomaterials exhibit vast potential for application in the lighting industry.

Xuanqi Hao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jianrong Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Chenchen Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Chuanhao Li: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ping Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Function of China, PR China, (grant no. 51972145), the Independent Innovation Team from Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, Jinan, China (grant no, 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112092.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
